message:
feat(block,core,fuse,mvcc): expand block layer, VFS operations, WAL hardening, and E2E harness

Block layer (ffs-block):
- Add 117 lines of new block device abstraction code including
  block-aligned I/O helpers, read/write coalescing, and error
  propagation improvements for the underlying storage layer

Core (ffs-core):
- Expand VFS operation set with new file metadata operations
  and directory entry helpers
- Add degradation mode enhancements for graceful handling of
  partial filesystem corruption
- Extend core type definitions for expanded block/inode operations

FUSE (ffs-fuse):
- Add new FUSE operation handlers for expanded VFS surface area
- Improve error mapping between FUSE error codes and internal
  FrankenFS error types

MVCC WAL (ffs-mvcc):
- Harden WAL write path with additional integrity checks
- Add WAL segment boundary validation to prevent cross-segment
  corruption during concurrent writes

Allocator (ffs-alloc):
- Minor bounds-checking improvement

E2E harness (ffs-harness):
- Expand test harness with additional scenario builders and
  assertion helpers for new block/VFS operations

Also updates AGENTS.md, README.md, and beads issue tracker.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1b7","title":"Implement btrfs COW B-tree mutation (insert/delete/split/merge)","description":"# Implement btrfs COW B-tree mutation (insert/delete/split/merge)

## GOAL
Implement the core btrfs COW B-tree mutation operations required for write path.

## BACKGROUND
btrfs uses COW semantics for all tree modifications:
- Never modify existing nodes in place
- Create new nodes with modifications
- Update parent pointers up to root
- Old root becomes part of transaction log

This is naturally compatible with MVCC version chains.

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Core Operations
```rust
pub trait BtrfsBTree {
    /// Insert key-value pair, returns new root
    fn insert(&self, key: BtrfsKey, item: &[u8], cx: &Cx) -> Result<BlockNumber>;
    
    /// Delete key, returns new root (or same if not found)
    fn delete(&self, key: &BtrfsKey, cx: &Cx) -> Result<BlockNumber>;
    
    /// Update existing key with new value
    fn update(&self, key: &BtrfsKey, item: &[u8], cx: &Cx) -> Result<BlockNumber>;
    
    /// Range query
    fn range(&self, start: &BtrfsKey, end: &BtrfsKey, cx: &Cx) 
        -> Result<Vec<(BtrfsKey, Vec<u8>)>>;
}
```

### 2. COW Mechanics
```rust
/// Clone-on-write a node with modification
fn cow_node(
    original: BlockNumber,
    modification: NodeMod,
    allocator: &dyn BtrfsAllocator,
    cx: &Cx,
) -> Result<BlockNumber> {
    // 1. Read original node
    // 2. Apply modification (insert/delete/update item)
    // 3. Allocate new block
    // 4. Write modified node to new block
    // 5. Return new block number
}
```

### 3. Split/Merge
- Split when node is full (> max items)
- Merge when node is underfull (< min items)
- Rebalance siblings before merge

### 4. Path Walking
- Track path from root to leaf for COW propagation
- Each level gets new block on modification
- Efficient path reuse for sequential inserts

## TESTS
1. Unit: Insert into empty tree
2. Unit: Insert causing split
3. Unit: Delete causing merge
4. Unit: COW produces new root
5. Integration: 1000 random inserts/deletes, tree invariants hold
6. Property: Random operations maintain sorted order

## LOGGING
- Tree modification (trace): operation, key, old root, new root
- Split/merge events (debug): node level, item counts
- Allocation (trace): new block numbers

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All operations preserve B-tree invariants
2. COW produces independent tree versions
3. No in-place modification of shared blocks
4. Performance: O(log N) for all operations","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T02:51:23.658366987Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:18.160085044Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:18.160001627Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btree","btrfs","core"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1b7","depends_on_id":"bd-22w.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:52:08.481147860Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1b7","depends_on_id":"bd-29z","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:56:39.683503676Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1b7","depends_on_id":"bd-375.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:52:08.388968210Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":48,"issue_id":"bd-1b7","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"BTRFS COW SEMANTICS: btrfs B-tree mutation is inherently COW—every modification creates a new node and updates the parent's pointer. This propagates up to the root, which gets a new generation number. This is fundamentally different from ext4 extent trees (in-place mutation + journal). The COW chain must be atomic: either all nodes in the chain are updated, or none are.","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:57:59Z"},{"id":59,"issue_id":"bd-1b7","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Progress update: added in-memory COW btrfs mutation core in crates/ffs-btrfs (BtrfsBTree trait + InMemoryCowBtrfsTree with insert/update/delete/range, split logic, root COW propagation, invariant validator, and deterministic random mutation test). Added tracing instrumentation for insert/split/delete paths. Full required gates pass (fmt/check/clippy/test workspace). Remaining gap before closure: optimize delete path from rebuild-based fallback to strict O(log N) merge/rebalance semantics and integrate allocator-backed block lifecycle for production write path wiring.","created_at":"2026-02-13T08:52:36Z"},{"id":60,"issue_id":"bd-1b7","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Validation note: full workspace gates currently blocked by concurrent non-bd-1b7 compile errors in crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs (E0502 at line ~6190, E0133 at line ~6412) and warning in crates/ffs-mvcc/src/sharded.rs. Package-local gates for ffs-btrfs pass (fmt, test, clippy). Current implementation is in place; remaining closure blocker is full-workspace gate failure from concurrent non-bd-1b7 edits (ffs-core/ffs-mvcc).","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:04:14Z"},{"id":63,"issue_id":"bd-1b7","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Claimed from bv robot-next and started closure triage.

- Delete path is already strict O(log N): delete_from + rebalance_child + rotate_from_left/right + merge_adjacent_nodes (no tree rebuild fallback in current implementation).
- Primary remaining closure gap is allocator-backed block lifecycle integration:
  - alloc_node currently only bumps in-memory next_block and inserts cloned nodes into nodes map.
  - No corresponding allocator-backed free path for superseded COW nodes.

- Sent coordination message in Agent Mail thread bd-1b7; will proceed with allocator lifecycle implementation once reservation is released/handed off.","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:17:04Z"},{"id":66,"issue_id":"bd-1b7","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Completed allocator-backed COW node lifecycle integration in crates/ffs-btrfs/src/lib.rs.

Implemented:
- Added `BtrfsAllocator` lifecycle interface and default `InMemoryBtrfsAllocator`.
- `InMemoryCowBtrfsTree` now supports custom allocator injection (`with_allocator`) and records deferred frees.
- Wired allocation through allocator (`alloc_node`) and deferred-free tracking (`retire_node`).
- Added deferred-free lifecycle hooks through mutation flow:
  - `insert_into` retires replaced node IDs.
  - `delete_from` retires replaced leaf/internal nodes.
  - `rebalance_child` retires replaced/merged sibling nodes.
  - `normalize_root_after_delete` retires collapsed root nodes.
- Added regression test: `cow_mutations_record_deferred_node_frees`.

Validation:

Closure note:
- Prior bead note cited delete-path fallback as a remaining concern; current implementation already uses O(log N) rebalance/merge, and this change closes the allocator lifecycle gap.","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:18Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1bx","title":"Implement degradation level display in TUI dashboard","description":"# Implement degradation level display in TUI dashboard

## GOAL
Add degradation level and pressure indicators to the TUI dashboard so operators can monitor system health.

## BACKGROUND
The graceful degradation system (bd-3tz) produces state that must be visible:
- Current degradation level (0-4)
- CPU/memory/IO pressure gauges
- Recent degradation events

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Extended DashboardSnapshot
```rust
pub struct DashboardSnapshot {
    // ... existing fields ...
    
    // Degradation state
    pub degradation_level: DegradationLevel,
    pub cpu_pressure: f64,      // 0.0 - 1.0
    pub memory_pressure: f64,   // 0.0 - 1.0
    pub io_queue_depth: usize,
    pub degradation_events: Vec<DegradationEvent>,
}
```

### 2. New Panel: System Health
```
┌─ System Health ─────────────────┐
│ Degradation: NORMAL (Level 0)   │
│                                 │
│ CPU:    [████████░░] 78%        │
│ Memory: [██████░░░░] 62%        │
│ I/O:    [███░░░░░░░] 34%        │
│                                 │
│ Recent events:                  │
│   12:34:05 Level 0 → 1 (CPU)    │
│   12:35:22 Level 1 → 0          │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 3. Color Coding
- Level 0 (Normal): Green
- Level 1 (Background paused): Yellow
- Level 2 (Cache reduced): Orange
- Level 3 (Writes throttled): Red
- Level 4 (Read-only): Blinking Red

### 4. Gauge Widgets
```rust
fn render_pressure_gauge(value: f64, width: u16) -> String {
    let filled = (value * width as f64) as u16;
    let bar = "█".repeat(filled as usize) + &"░".repeat((width - filled) as usize);
    format!("[{}] {:>3}%", bar, (value * 100.0) as u8)
}
```

## TESTS
1. Unit: Gauge rendering at various values
2. Unit: Color coding for each level
3. Integration: Dashboard updates on level change
4. Visual: Screenshot test for layout

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Degradation level always visible
2. Pressure gauges update in real-time
3. Event history shows last 10 events
4.  {"id":"bd-3tz","title":"EPIC: Graceful Degradation & Backpressure","description":"# EPIC: Graceful Degradation & Backpressure

## PURPOSE
Implement automated graceful degradation under compute pressure, ensuring FrankenFS remains responsive even on overloaded machines.

## BACKGROUND
From alien-artifact-coding principles:
- Systems must degrade gracefully, not catastrophically
- Backpressure mechanisms prevent cascade failures
- Adaptive budgets allow runtime tuning

Key scenarios:
1. CPU saturation (high system load)
2. Memory pressure (approaching OOM)
3. I/O bottleneck (slow disk/FUSE overhead)
4. Concurrent request surge

## DESIGN PRINCIPLES (Alien-Artifact Quality)

### 1. Adaptive Budget System
- Every operation has a configurable budget (time, CPU cycles, memory)
- Budgets combine: deadline=min, quota=min, priority=max
- Operations shed load when budget exhausted

### 2. Backpressure Propagation
- FUSE request queue depth limiting
- ARC cache eviction under memory pressure
- Transaction queue throttling
- Scrub/GC pause under load

### 3. Graceful Degradation Hierarchy
Level 0: Full functionality (normal operation)
Level 1: Defer background tasks (scrub, GC paused)
Level 2: Reduce cache size (evict cold entries)
Level 3: Reject new write transactions (reads continue)
Level 4: Read-only mode (emergency)

### 4. Observability
- Degradation level exposed in TUI dashboard
- Prometheus-compatible metrics export
- Structured logging for degradation events

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Under 16x CPU load, read latency increases <5x (not infinite)
2. Under memory pressure, OOM killer is NOT invoked
3. Degradation state visible in dashboard
4. Recovery to normal when pressure relieved
5. All degradation events logged with evidence

## IMPLEMENTATION FILES
- crates/ffs-core/src/backpressure.rs (new)
- crates/ffs-block/src/lib.rs (cache pressure hooks)
- crates/ffs-mvcc/src/lib.rs (transaction throttling)
- crates/ffs-tui/src/lib.rs (degradation panel)

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. E2E degradation test (bd-lqwc) passes
3. Degradation levels transition correctly with hysteresis
4. Foreground read latency < 2x baseline under pressure
5.  {"id":"bd-3tz.1","title":"Implement compute budget propagation through Cx context","description":"# Implement compute budget propagation through Cx context

## GOAL
Extend the asupersync Cx capability context to carry a "compute budget" that enables automatic graceful degradation when the system is under pressure.

## BACKGROUND
From AGENTS.md: "automated graceful degradation in the face of compute pressure (like an overloaded machine)."

The idea: every Cx carries a budget (0.0 to 1.0 representing available compute headroom). When the machine is overloaded:
1. Budget decreases
2. Operations check budget and degrade gracefully:
   - Reduce ARC cache size (evict more aggressively)
   - Increase scrub interval (scan less frequently)
   - Batch FUSE responses (reduce syscall overhead)
   - Skip non-essential checksums (degrade to faster but weaker verification)
   - Reduce MVCC version retention (prune more aggressively)

## DESIGN
```rust
pub struct ComputeBudget {
    headroom: f64,        // 0.0 = critically overloaded, 1.0 = idle
    update_interval: Duration,
    source: BudgetSource, // LoadAvg, CpuUsage, IoWait, Composite
}

impl ComputeBudget {
    pub fn sample() -> f64 { /* read /proc/loadavg or equivalent */ }
    pub fn should_degrade(&self, threshold: f64) -> bool { self.headroom < threshold }
}
```

Every subsystem registers a degradation policy:
```rust
trait DegradationPolicy {
    fn apply(&self, budget: f64);  // Called when budget changes
    fn describe(&self) -> &str;     // Human-readable policy name
}
```

## DELIVERABLES
1. ComputeBudget struct with Linux load average sampling
2. Budget propagation through Cx (attached as context value)
3. DegradationPolicy trait
4. Concrete policies for: ARC cache, scrub daemon, MVCC retention
5. Budget monitor Region (samples periodically, updates Cx)

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: Budget reads load average on Linux
2. Unit: Budget calculates correct headroom (high load = low headroom)
3. Unit: Degradation triggers when budget drops below threshold
4. Unit: Recovery when budget returns above threshold
5. Unit: Multiple policies compose correctly (all triggered on change)
6. Integration: Simulated high load triggers all policies
7. Integration: Policy changes visible in TUI dashboard

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
### Budget monitoring
- budget_sample (trace): raw_load, cpu_count, calculated_headroom
- budget_change (debug): old_headroom, new_headroom, direction (up/down)
- budget_level (info): level (normal/warning/critical), headroom (on level change only)

### Degradation policy logging
- policy_register (debug): policy_name, threshold
- policy_triggered (info): policy_name, action, old_state, new_state
- policy_recovery (info): policy_name, recovered_to_state

### Pressure source logging
- load_avg_read (trace): 1min, 5min, 15min
- cpu_usage_read (trace): user_pct, system_pct, iowait_pct
- memory_pressure (trace): available_mb, used_pct

### Error logging
- budget_sample_failed (warn): error (e.g., /proc/loadavg read failed)
- policy_apply_failed (error): policy_name, error

### Metrics (for TUI)
- current_headroom: Gauge (0.0 to 1.0)
- degradation_level: Gauge (0-4)
- active_policies: Gauge (count of triggered policies)

## DEGRADATION LEVELS
```
Level 0: headroom >= 0.5 (Normal)
Level 1: headroom >= 0.3 (Warning - background tasks paused)
Level 2: headroom >= 0.15 (Degraded - cache reduced)
Level 3: headroom >= 0.05 (Critical - writes throttled)
Level 4: headroom < 0.05 (Emergency - read-only mode)
```

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. System degrades gracefully under load (no OOM, no stalls)
2. Recovery is automatic when pressure subsides
3. All degradation decisions logged for observability
4. TUI dashboard shows current degradation state
5. No false positives (brief load spikes don't cause oscillation)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:53:02.830810925Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T19:57:45.378286190Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T19:57:45.378257696Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3tz.1","depends_on_id":"bd-3tz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:53:02.830810925Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":38,"issue_id":"bd-3tz.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ALIEN-ARTIFACT QUALITY: Compute budget propagation should use anytime-valid monitoring (sequential testing) rather than fixed thresholds.  {"id":"bd-3tz.2","title":"Cross-crate integration: backpressure gates scrub daemon and GC scheduling","description":"# Cross-crate integration: backpressure gates scrub daemon and GC scheduling

## GOAL
Wire the backpressure/degradation system (bd-3tz) to throttle background scrub (bd-15c.5) and EBR GC (bd-14jb) when the system is under compute pressure. Background tasks must yield to foreground I/O.

## BACKGROUND
The backpressure system (bd-2r8) propagates compute budget through Cx context. Background daemons (scrub, GC, flush) must respect this budget. Currently no bead wires these together.

## DELIVERABLES
1. ScrubDaemon checks Cx compute budget before scanning each group
2. EBR GC checks Cx budget before each reclamation batch
3. FlushDaemon reduces batch size under pressure
4. All three daemons yield when budget is exhausted (resume when pressure relieved)

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: scrub daemon yields when budget is zero
2. Unit: GC skips batch when budget < threshold
3. Integration: under simulated pressure, background tasks reduce throughput
4. Integration: foreground I/O latency stays within bounds under pressure

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Daemon throttled (debug): daemon_name, budget_remaining, yield_duration_ms
- Daemon resumed (debug): daemon_name, new_budget
- Batch size reduced (debug): daemon_name, original_size, reduced_size, pressure_level

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Foreground read latency < 2x baseline under heavy background load
2. Background tasks eventually complete (no indefinite starvation)
3.  {"id":"bd-5s0r","title":"EPIC: Write-Back Cache","description":"# EPIC: Write-Back Cache

## PURPOSE
Implement a write-back cache layer between FUSE write operations and disk I/O. This is critical for write performance — without write-back caching, every FUSE write would require immediate disk I/O (write-through), making FrankenFS orders of magnitude slower than kernel filesystems.

## DESIGN

### Phased Approach
1. DirtyTracker: track which cached blocks have been modified but not flushed
2. FlushDaemon: background thread that periodically flushes dirty blocks to disk
3. MVCC Integration: dirty block tracking integrates with MVCC version chains

### Core Invariant
**Dirty blocks must never be evicted from cache until flushed to disk.**
Violating this invariant causes data loss (dirty data discarded without writing).

### DirtyTracker
- Per-block dirty flag in cache metadata
- Dirty list: ordered by age (oldest dirty block first)
- Statistics: dirty block count, dirty data size, oldest dirty age

### FlushDaemon
- Background thread wakes periodically (configurable: default 5s)
- Flushes oldest dirty blocks first (LRU-like)
- Flush batch size: configurable (default 256 blocks)
- Integrates with fsync: explicit fsync flushes all dirty blocks for that inode
- Backpressure: if dirty ratio exceeds threshold (e.g., 80%), block new writes until flushed

### MVCC Integration
- Dirty blocks belong to a specific MVCC version
- Flush writes the committed version (not in-flight versions)
- Aborted transaction: dirty blocks discarded without flush
- Committed transaction: dirty blocks eligible for flush

### Eviction Coordination
- Cache eviction (ARC or S3-FIFO) must check dirty flag before evicting
- If dirty: flush first, then evict
- If flush fails: do NOT evict (keep dirty block in cache, retry later)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Dirty blocks tracked correctly (set on write, cleared on flush)
- [ ] FlushDaemon runs in background, flushes oldest dirty blocks
- [ ] fsync flushes all dirty blocks for specified inode
- [ ] Dirty blocks never evicted without flush (invariant enforced)
- [ ] Backpressure prevents OOM from too many dirty blocks
- [ ] MVCC integration: only committed versions flushed
- [ ] Benchmark: write throughput with write-back vs write-through

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. E2E durability test (bd-5s0r.4) passes
3. Dirty blocks never evicted without flush (invariant enforced)
4. Write throughput >= 80pct of kernel ext4 write-back
5. fsync flushes all dirty blocks for target inode","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:26:02.706174558Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-17T23:46:05.116910898Z","closed_at":"2026-02-17T23:46:05.116892123Z","close_reason":"All children closed.
